title: Polynomially Effective Equidistribution for Some Higher Dimensional Unipotent Subgroups
speaker: Zuo Lin
abstract: Let G be a perfect Lie group, Γ be a lattice in G and U be a unipotent subgroup of G. A celebrated theorem of Ratner says that for any x in G/Γ the orbit U.x is equidistributed in a periodic orbit of some subgroup U≤L≤G. Establishing a quantitative version of Ratner's theorem has been long sought after. If U is a horospherical subgroup of G, the question is well-studied. If U is not a horospherical subgroup, this question is far less understood. Recently, Lindenstrauss, Mohammadi, Wang and Yang established fully quantitative and effective equidistribution results for orbits of one-parameter (non-horospherical) unipotent groups in a wide variety of cases. In this talk, we will discuss a recent equidistribution theorem for some higher dimensional unipotent subgroups. Our results in particular provide effective equidistribution theorems for orbits of maximal unipotent subgroups of SO(p,q) in SLn(ℝ)/SLn(ℤ) for all n=p+q. If time permits, we will also discuss a submodularity inequality in irreducible representations, which is a key ingredient of the proof and is of independent interest.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Ratner baseline and the effective problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Ratner's orbit-closure and equidistribution theorem for unipotent flows and subgroups, then formulates the need for polynomially effective rates with unavoidable obstructions from nearby small periodic orbits.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic homogeneous setting and nondegeneracy hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes an arithmetic homogeneous space G/Gamma, a semisimple subgroup H, a diagonal flow a_t in H, the associated expanding unipotent subgroup inside H, and irreducibility/proximal-type assumptions on the H-complement in the Lie algebra.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial effective equidistribution dichotomy
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that for the specified data, an expanding U-average either equidistributes to Haar measure with polynomial error against Sobolev test functions or the base point is polynomially close to a small H-periodic orbit.

proof_stages.label: Literature scope and applications
proof_stages.summary: The result is positioned among previous effective horospherical, semidirect, random-walk, periodic-orbit, Oppenheim, and principal-SL_2 results, and its applications to effective Oppenheim and dense lattice-orbit averages are described.

proof_stages.label: Expanding-average and transverse-dimension framework
proof_stages.summary: The proof is organized by replacing large unipotent averages with expanding pieces that decompose into many random-walk steps and by measuring coarse dimension or entropy in directions transverse to the H-dynamics.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic initial transverse dimension
proof_stages.summary: The first proof phase obtains a positive starting amount of transverse dimension using arithmeticity and an effective closing lemma for long unipotent trajectories or large unipotent balls.

proof_stages.label: Projection and entropy dimension amplification
proof_stages.summary: The central proof phase raises the transverse dimension to almost full dimension by proving projection inequalities for irreducible H-representations, handling degeneracies through conditional entropy submodularity and an induction on intersections of moved subspaces.

proof_stages.label: Spectral-gap endgame to equidistribution
proof_stages.summary: Once the transverse dimension is nearly full, a spectral-gap argument on the ambient homogeneous space, combined with Bourgain-type sparsity/equidistribution ideas, converts dimension growth into the polynomial equidistribution alternative.
